speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back. Today we're doing something that sounds dramatic but is actually very ordinary in Git and GitHub: surviving a merge conflict.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I appreciate that wording, because "merge conflict" sounds like I broke the internet.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. But a merge conflict is not a failure. It's a checkpoint. Git is simply saying, "Two changes overlap, and I need a human decision."

speaker: ALEX
text: Here's the big picture. In this lesson, we'll cover what a merge conflict is, why it happens, how to prevent it, how to read the conflict markers, how to resolve conflicts on GitHub, how VS Code helps, what to do in the terminal if you work locally, and what to do when you feel stuck.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for this course specifically, there's a controlled practice challenge, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. This chapter uses one short practice challenge so you can learn conflict resolution in a low-pressure way. The pattern is: observe, resolve, verify. The time expectation is under ten minutes. Your evidence is an issue-linked pull request and a completion comment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the goal isn't just "make the warning go away." It's to understand the structure, make a decision, and prove you resolved it cleanly.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's it.

speaker: ALEX
text: For the challenge, your facilitator triggers a merge conflict in your pull request. You open the PR, look for the conflict notice, choose Resolve conflicts or edit the file directly on your branch, find the conflict markers, decide what content belongs in the final file, delete the marker lines, and commit the resolution.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the autograder is looking for two things: no conflict markers left behind, and meaningful content still in the file.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. So if you remove everything, that's not success. And if you leave even one marker line, that's not success either.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's define the core idea. A merge conflict happens when two people change the same part of the same file in different ways, and Git cannot automatically decide which version to keep.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if I edit line twelve and you edit line two hundred, Git usually handles that fine.

speaker: ALEX
text: Usually, yes. But if we both edit the same line, or nearby lines in a way that overlaps, Git may stop and ask for help.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What are the common reasons this happens?

speaker: ALEX
text: A few classics: two people edited the same line, one person deleted a file while another edited it, two people restructured the same section, or a branch stayed open too long and drifted far away from main.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That last one feels especially realistic in open source.

speaker: ALEX
text: Very realistic. Long-running branches invite conflicts.

speaker: ALEX
text: Before we resolve conflicts, let's make them less likely. Prevention is easier than resolution.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give us the practical list.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep branches short-lived. Aim for small, focused work that merges in a few days, not a few weeks. Sync with main frequently. From your feature branch, you may merge origin slash main into your branch. And if you're comfortable with rebasing, rebasing onto origin slash main is another option. Communicate with your team. If you know you'll be editing a heavily used file, say so. Avoid mass reformatting unless it's coordinated, because changing lots of lines creates unnecessary overlap. Pull before you push, so you're not building on stale history. Work on separate files when possible. Keep pull requests small. And use draft pull requests for early visibility, so other contributors can see what area you're touching before things collide.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a strong pattern: short branches, frequent sync, small PRs, visible work.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Those habits reduce stress.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there was also an advanced prevention idea about fast-forward merges.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The important concept is that simpler branch histories can reduce complexity. You don't need to master fast-forward behavior today, but it helps to know that the shape of history affects how cleanly changes integrate.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Okay. Now let's talk about the thing learners actually see: conflict markers.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. When Git can't merge automatically, it inserts plain-text markers directly into the file. Here is the classic shape: Less-than signs repeated seven times, then HEAD. Then one version of the content. Then a separator line made of equals signs. Then the other version of the content. Then greater-than signs repeated seven times, followed by a branch name.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And because this is an audio lesson, let's label the sections clearly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Good idea. There are three important parts. First, the section between the start marker and the separator. Second, the section between the separator and the end marker. Third, the marker lines themselves. In the solution reference, the section between the start marker and the separator is described as the version on the branch you are merging into, usually main. The section between the separator and the end marker is the version on your branch.

speaker: JAMIE
text: But in the practice chapter instructions, the wording says the first block is your version, the current branch, and the second block is their version, the incoming branch.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right, and that's why context matters. Different tools and workflows describe the sides a little differently depending on where the merge is happening. The safe habit is this: do not rely only on labels like "yours" and "theirs." Read the actual text on both sides and decide what final content should remain.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's helpful, especially for learners who worry about memorizing the wrong mapping.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The universally safe truth is: the marker lines are not content, and the final file must contain only the text you actually want to keep.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's make that concrete with the example resolution from the reference solution. Suppose one side says, "Welcome to the Learning Room. This is the main branch version." The other side says, "Welcome to the Learning Room. This is my improved version with more detail." Your options are threefold. Keep yours: delete the other section and all marker lines. Keep theirs: delete your section and all marker lines. Or combine both: write one clean final version that incorporates the best parts of each.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So there is not always one magic correct answer.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. The decision depends on what is better for the project. Two valid resolutions may use different wording.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That fits the peer simulation note too. It's okay if your final wording differs from someone else's, as long as it's a valid resolution.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly right.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now let's walk through the challenge flow step by step. First, wait for your facilitator to trigger the conflict. If you don't see a conflict yet, it may simply not be ready. Second, open your assigned challenge issue. It tells you which practice file contains the conflict markers. Third, open that file and search for the start marker. In a browser you can use Control F. In VS Code you can also search directly. Screen reader users can search for the literal characters, since the markers are plain text and fully readable. Fourth, read both sides carefully. Fifth, decide: keep one side or combine them. Sixth, delete all three types of marker lines: the start line, the equals separator, and the end line with the branch name. Seventh, review the file and search again so there are zero remaining markers. Eighth, commit your changes on your short-lived feature branch. The recommended naming pattern is fix slash your name dash issue number. Ninth, open or update your pull request. The suggested title pattern is fix: resolve conflict markers in filename. In the body, include Closes number, with your issue number, and a brief sentence or two explaining what content you kept and why.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And when everything is open and passing checks, there's one more administrative step.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Post a completion comment on your assigned challenge issue. The example format is: Chapter 7 completed, then the PR number, the filename, and your content decision with a short reason.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That matters because this course wants issue-linked evidence, not just a private sense that "I think I fixed it."

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's stay with accessibility for a moment. What should blind or low-vision learners know about navigating this?

speaker: ALEX
text: A few key points. The conflict markers themselves are just text, so screen readers can read them directly. Search is your friend. Searching for the start marker is often the fastest way to jump into the conflict area. If you're on GitHub and can't find the Resolve conflicts button, look on the PR conversation tab near the merge section toward the bottom. Screen reader users may want to search the page for the phrase "Resolve conflicts" button. For low-vision users, high contrast themes and zoom can make the conflict regions easier to parse, especially in editors that highlight current and incoming changes. And this chapter also includes learning card guidance for different working styles: visual and mouse users, low vision users, NVDA or JAWS on Windows, VoiceOver on macOS, and CLI users.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the chapter is intentionally not one-size-fits-all.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Open the guidance that matches how you work.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's talk about spotting a conflict on GitHub. On a pull request, GitHub may show a message that the branch has conflicts that must be resolved before merging.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And that's your cue that GitHub needs human help.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. If the web editor is available, choose Resolve conflicts. GitHub's conflict editor will show the conflicting sections, and your task is to edit the file into one clean final version. Step by step on GitHub: click Resolve conflicts, edit the file so the markers are removed and the final content is what you want, choose Mark as resolved, and then Commit merge.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does the editor generally feel like?

speaker: ALEX
text: It highlights the conflict region and places you right where the disagreement is. But the same rule applies: don't just click through. Read both sides. Decide intentionally.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now compare that with VS Code.

speaker: ALEX
text: In VS Code, conflicts are often shown with highlighted regions and helpful actions above each conflict. Common actions include Accept Current Change, Accept Incoming Change, and Accept Both Changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And we should translate those a little.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. "Current" usually means the version currently checked out in your workspace. "Incoming" usually means the version being merged in. "Accept Both" keeps both chunks, after which you may still need to edit for clarity and formatting.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So even when the tool gives you buttons, you still need judgment.

speaker: ALEX
text: Always. Tooling helps, but it doesn't replace understanding.

speaker: ALEX
text: If you cloned the repository and prefer local work, there is a terminal-based alternative. You update main, switch back to your branch, and merge main into your branch. If a conflict happens, Git pauses the merge and reports the affected files. Then you open each conflicted file in your editor, look for the markers, remove the markers while keeping the content you want, save the file, mark it resolved with git add, complete the merge with git commit, and push. Your pull request updates automatically.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can we also walk through the command-line conflict reading pattern more explicitly?

speaker: ALEX
text: Definitely. Start the merge that causes the conflict. Then check which files have conflicts. Look for lines like "both modified" in Git's output. Open each conflicted file in your editor. Edit the file: remove the marker lines, keep the content you want. After editing, mark the file as resolved with git add. Then complete the merge with git commit. Git often auto-fills the merge commit message. After that, check the result. Review the diff so you understand what changed. On the PR, review merge state, checks, and overall readiness.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That sequence really matters: detect, edit, add, commit, verify.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There was also a real-file style example in the chapter, where two students both add content to the same table row.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, and that example is useful because it shows that conflicts aren't always huge. Sometimes two people just edited the same small region, like one row in a Markdown table. The resolution options remain the same: keep one edit, keep the other, or combine them into one clean row that preserves the useful information.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the skill scales from tiny wording changes to larger sections.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now, something important philosophically: conflicts are not always bad.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That sounds surprising.

speaker: ALEX
text: A conflict can be evidence that two contributors both cared about the same important area. The conflict forces a deliberate review. In that sense, it can improve quality, because someone must consciously choose the final wording or logic rather than letting one change silently overwrite the other.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the chapter also frames this as relevant to AI-assisted work.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Sometimes an AI tool confidently produces changes that overlap with human-written changes. Conflict resolution becomes part of supervising AI safely. You are not just merging text. You are evaluating which content deserves to survive.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's cover the most common stuck moments, because learners definitely hit these.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. If you do not see a conflict, your facilitator may not have triggered it yet. Wait for the signal. If you cannot find the Resolve conflicts button, check the PR conversation area near the merge section, or search the page for that button label. If you are not sure what to keep, read both versions aloud. If both are useful, combine them. There is often no single correct answer. If the autograder still reports conflict markers, search the file for all three marker types. Every one of them must be completely removed. If you accidentally deleted too much, undo and redo that section carefully. And if you want a sanity check, ask your facilitator before opening the PR.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about the nuclear option?

speaker: ALEX
text: Also valid. If you feel tangled up, you can abandon the attempt and start fresh from a clean copy of the branch or file. Sometimes restarting is faster than untangling confused edits.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's reassuring. Starting over is not cheating.

speaker: ALEX
text: Not at all.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's do a compact checklist. A merge conflict means overlapping edits need a human decision. Conflict markers are plain text and come in three parts: start marker, separator, end marker. Read both sides. Choose one side or combine them. Delete all marker lines. Search again to confirm none remain. Commit the resolution. Verify the PR status, checks, and diff. Submit your issue-linked evidence.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And emotionally?

speaker: ALEX
text: Conflicts are normal. They are manageable. The skill is calm reading and intentional editing.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we wrap up, what are the expected outcomes for this chapter?

speaker: ALEX
text: You should be able to identify the three marker lines immediately, read both sides of a conflict, make an intentional content decision, remove all markers, and submit a clean pull request linked to the issue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And that's the learning moment, really. Not "avoid all conflicts forever," but "recognize them without panic and resolve them clearly."

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. That is the point.

speaker: ALEX
text: If you want to check current official guidance beyond the course material, the authoritative sources named for this chapter are GitHub Docs, the GitHub Changelog, GitHub documentation about Git and pull requests, GitHub Flow guidance, and the Git SCM book's discussion of basic merge conflicts.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the course teaches the workflow, and those references are your source of truth when interfaces change.

speaker: ALEX
text: Well said.

speaker: ALEX
text: One final reminder for the challenge: your job is not to preserve the markers. Your job is to produce a clean file that says what the project should actually say.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Delete the markers, keep the meaning.

speaker: ALEX
text: Perfect. And once you can do that, merge conflicts stop feeling scary.

speaker: JAMIE
text: They start feeling like editing.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.
